items.title: Diagnosis of Irritable Bowel Syndrome Using Rome IV Criteria
items.content: IBS is diagnosed based on clinical presentation due to the absence of clear diagnostic markers. The Rome IV criteria require recurrent abdominal pain at least once per week for the last three months, associated with two or more specific stool-related symptoms.
keyPoints: No clear diagnostic markers exist for IBS; diagnosis relies on clinical presentation.
Rome IV criteria: Recurrent abdominal pain ≥1 day/week in the last 3 months.
Pain must be associated with ≥2 of the following: related to defecation, change in stool frequency, or change in stool form.
~10% of adults and adolescents globally have symptoms consistent with IBS.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome

items.title: Epidemiology and Impact of Irritable Bowel Syndrome
items.content: IBS affects approximately 10% of the global adult and adolescent population. The condition's symptoms can overlap with other disorders, leading to significant impairment in quality of life and high healthcare costs.
keyPoints: ~10% of adults and adolescents worldwide have IBS symptoms.
Symptoms fluctuate over time and may overlap with fibromyalgia, headache, backache, and genitourinary symptoms.
Severity varies, significantly impacting quality of life and resulting in high healthcare costs.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome

items.title: Diagnosis of Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS) Requires Abdominal Pain Associated with Defecation or Stool Changes
items.content: For diagnosing IBS, abdominal pain is essential and must be associated with defecation or changes in stool frequency/form. The Rome IV criteria specify that this pain should occur at least once a week.
keyPoints: Abdominal pain is necessary for IBS diagnosis.
Pain must be linked to defecation or changes in stool frequency/form.
Rome IV requires abdominal pain at least weekly.
Painless diarrhea or constipation does not meet IBS criteria.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: IBS Bowel Habit Alterations: Subtypes and Instability
items.content: Altered bowel habits are a consistent feature of IBS, leading to classifications such as IBS-C (constipation), IBS-D (diarrhea), and IBS-M (mixed). These subtypes are unstable, with 75% of patients changing subtypes over one year.
keyPoints: Altered bowel habits are a key feature of IBS.
IBS can be classified into IBS-C, IBS-D, and IBS-M based on predominant symptoms.
~33% prevalence rates for each subtype (IBS-D, IBS-C, IBS-M).
75% of patients change subtypes annually; 29% switch between IBS-C and IBS-D.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Upper GI Symptoms in IBS: High Overlap with Dyspepsia
items.content: 25-50% of IBS patients experience upper GI symptoms like dyspepsia, heartburn, and nausea. There is a significant overlap between IBS and functional dyspepsia, suggesting they may be manifestations of a broader digestive disorder.
keyPoints: 25-50% of IBS patients report upper GI symptoms.
Overlap with dyspepsia: 31.7% prevalence in dyspeptic patients vs. 7.9% without.
55.6% of IBS patients have dyspepsia symptoms.
IBS and functional dyspepsia may represent a single digestive disorder.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: GI Motor Abnormalities in IBS
items.content: IBS patients exhibit increased colonic motor activity under stimulated conditions, such as after eating or rectal balloon inflation. This is characterized by prolonged contractile responses and rapid transit associated with abdominal pain.
keyPoints: Increased rectosigmoid motor activity for up to 3 hours post-meal
Marked distention-evoked contractile activity in IBS-D and IBS-C patients
Motility index and peak amplitude of HAPCs greatly increased in IBS-D compared to healthy subjects
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Visceral Hypersensitivity in IBS
items.content: IBS patients have exaggerated sensory responses to visceral stimulation, with lower thresholds for sensations from rectal balloon inflation and lipid ingestion. This suggests a selective dysfunction of visceral afferents.
keyPoints: Food intolerance perceptions are at least two-fold more common than in the general population
Rectal balloon inflation produces sensations at lower volumes in IBS patients
Lipids lower thresholds for gas, discomfort, and pain sensations
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Central Neural Dysregulation in IBS
items.content: IBS is associated with CNS factors, including emotional disorders and stress. Functional MRI shows greater activation of the mid-cingulate cortex and prefrontal lobe during colonic stimulation, indicating hypervigilance to visceral pain.
keyPoints: Greater activation of the mid-cingulate cortex in response to distal colonic stimulation
Prefrontal lobe activation linked to increased alertness
Emotional disorders and stress are clinically associated with IBS
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Role of Mast Cells in IBS Pathogenesis
items.content: Mast cells are implicated in the pathogenesis of visceral hypersensitivity in IBS through their proximity to sensory nerves and release of mediators like histamine, proteases, and prostaglandin E2. This activation correlates with abdominal pain severity.
keyPoints: Persistent signs of low-grade mucosal inflammation in some IBS patients
Abnormal release of proinflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-1β, TNF) from PBMCs
Electromicroscopic evidence of mast cell activation in colonic mucosa
Correlation between activated mast cells and submucosal nerve fibers with abdominal pain frequency and severity
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: TRPV1 Channels and Visceral Hypersensitivity in IBS
items.content: Transient receptor potential vanilloid type 1 (TRPV1) channels are central to the initiation and persistence of visceral hypersensitivity in IBS, with increased expression correlating with symptoms.
keyPoints: Mucosal inflammation leads to increased TRPV1 expression/sensitization
Enhanced TRPV1 expression correlates with visceral hypersensitivity and abdominal pain
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Altered Intestinal Permeability in IBS
items.content: A subset of IBS patients, particularly those with IBS-D and postinfectious IBS, exhibit impaired intestinal barrier function, characterized by decreased tight junction protein expression and increased macromolecule passage.
keyPoints: Impaired gut epithelial lining in some IBS patients
Correlation between altered permeability and symptom severity
Structural changes like decreased tight junction proteins
Functional consequences such as increased passage of macromolecules
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Abnormal Serotonin Pathways in IBS-D
items.content: In a subset of IBS-D patients, there is an increase in serotonin-containing enterochromaffin cells and postprandial plasma serotonin levels compared to healthy controls. This abnormality may be linked to functional TPH1 polymorphism and gut microbial influences on serotonin production.
keyPoints: Increased serotonin-containing enterochromaffin cells in IBS-D patients
Higher postprandial plasma serotonin levels in IBS-D patients
Functional TPH1 polymorphism associated with IBS subtypes
Gut microbes affect colonic serotonin production via short-chain fatty acids
Downregulated SERT expression due to gram-negative gut dysbiosis
Increased serotonin contributes to postprandial symptoms
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.title: Bile Acids and IBS-D Pathophysiology
items.content: Up to 25% of IBS-D patients have idiopathic bile acid diarrhea, characterized by increased hepatic synthesis and colonic bile acid levels. This can result from reduced FGF-19 synthesis or genetic variations affecting bile acid feedback mechanisms.
keyPoints: 25% of IBS-D patients exhibit idiopathic bile acid diarrhea
Increased hepatic synthesis and colonic bile acid levels
Reduced FGF-19 synthesis by ileal mucosa
Genetic variation in proteins involved in negative feedback of bile acid synthesis
Increased colonic bile acids accelerate colonic transit, intestinal permeability, and visceral hypersensitivity
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.title: Diagnosis and Differentiation of IBS
items.content: IBS diagnosis relies on clinical features and exclusion of organic diseases, using Rome IV criteria which focus on abdominal pain and altered bowel habits. A careful history and physical examination are crucial.
keyPoints: Rome IV criteria published in 2016 define IBS based on abdominal pain and altered bowel habits.

Clinical features suggestive of IBS include recurrent lower abdominal pain with altered bowel habits, onset during stress or emotional upset, absence of systemic symptoms like fever and weight loss, and small-volume stool without blood.

Red flags against IBS diagnosis: first appearance in old age, progressive course, rectal bleeding, significant weight loss, persistent diarrhea after 48-h fast, nocturnal diarrhea, or steatorrheic stools.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 1)

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of IBS
items.content: IBS symptoms overlap with many GI disorders; differentiating based on pain quality, location, and timing is essential. Considerations include biliary tract disease, peptic ulcers, intestinal ischemia, carcinomas, diverticular disease, inflammatory bowel diseases, gastroparesis, small intestinal bacteria overgrowth, parasitic infections, lactase deficiency, laxative abuse, malabsorption, celiac sprue, hyperthyroidism, and drug-induced constipation.
keyPoints: Differential diagnosis includes biliary tract disease, peptic ulcer disorders, intestinal ischemia, carcinomas of the stomach/pancreas, diverticular disease, IBD, gastroparesis, small intestinal bacteria overgrowth, Giardia lamblia infection, lactase deficiency, laxative abuse, malabsorption, celiac sprue, hyperthyroidism, infectious diarrhea, drug-induced constipation, hypothyroidism, hypoparathyroidism, acute intermittent porphyria, and lead poisoning.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Evaluation for IBS
items.content: Minimal testing is needed for typical IBS without alarm features. The evaluation's aggressiveness depends on symptom duration, changes over time, patient age and sex, referral status, prior studies, family history of colorectal malignancy, and psychosocial dysfunction.
keyPoints: Typical IBS patients with no alarm features require minimal testing.
Complete blood count and fecal calprotectin (or lactoferrin), C-reactive protein checks are recommended for IBS-D to rule out IBD.
Serologic testing for celiac disease is advised if its prevalence in the general population is 1% or higher.
Stool examination for ova and parasites is not necessary unless parasitic infections are endemic/common or history suggests a possibility.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 1)

items.subtopic: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 2) | PATIENT COUNSELING AND DIETARY ALTERATIONS | STOOL-BULKING AGENTS
items.title: When to Consider Colonoscopy and Biopsies in IBS Patients
items.content: Colonoscopy is warranted for IBS patients with alarm features, symptom onset after age 40, or lack of response to traditional therapies. Random biopsies are recommended if persistent or chronic diarrhea is present to rule out microscopic colitis.
keyPoints: Alarm features or symptom onset after age 40 necessitate colonoscopy.
Lack of response to antidiarrheals also warrants colonoscopy.
Random biopsies should be performed for persistent or chronic diarrhea to exclude microscopic colitis.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 2) | PATIENT COUNSELING AND DIETARY ALTERATIONS | STOOL-BULKING AGENTS

items.subtopic: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 2) | PATIENT COUNSELING AND DIETARY ALTERATIONS | STOOL-BULKING AGENTS
items.title: Role of Fiber in Managing IBS Symptoms
items.content: Soluble fiber, such as psyllium, is effective for managing some IBS symptoms by increasing stool bulk and improving colonic transit. It should be started at a low dose and gradually increased to 20–30 g per day.
keyPoints: Fiber supplementation can help with constipation and bloating in IBS.
Psyllium, a soluble fiber, reduces perception of rectal distention.
Start fiber at a nominal dose, titrate up over weeks to 20–30 g/day.
Insoluble fibers like wheat bran are not supported for IBS management.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 2) | PATIENT COUNSELING AND DIETARY ALTERATIONS | STOOL-BULKING AGENTS

items.title: Optimal Timing and Choice for Antispasmodics in IBS Management
items.content: Anticholinergic drugs provide temporary relief from painful cramps by inhibiting the gastrocolic reflex. For effective pain management, these should be administered 30 minutes before meals to achieve optimal blood levels. While natural belladonna alkaloids are common, synthetic options like dicyclomine may offer fewer side effects. Peppermint oil is another option, showing significant improvement in IBS symptoms and abdominal pain reduction compared to placebo.
keyPoints: Anticholinergic drugs inhibit the gastrocolic reflex; administer 30 min before meals for best effect.
Natural belladonna alkaloids may cause xerostomia, urinary hesitancy/retention, blurred vision, and drowsiness.
Synthetic anticholinergics like dicyclomine have fewer side effects.
Peppermint oil significantly improves IBS symptoms and reduces abdominal pain (meta-analysis of 10 trials with 1030 patients).
Most common adverse event from peppermint oil is mild, transient heartburn.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTISPASMODICS | ANTIDIARRHEAL AGENTS

items.title: Loperamide and Eluxadoline in IBS-D Management: Indications and Precautions
items.content: For IBS with diarrhea (IBS-D), peripherally acting opiate-based agents like loperamide are preferred. Loperamide is effective at doses of 2–4 mg every 4–6 hours, up to a maximum of 12 mg/day. It is less addictive than other opiates and does not require dose escalation for efficacy. Eluxadoline, approved by the FDA, targets abdominal pain and diarrhea but carries a risk of pancreatitis; it should be avoided in patients with prior cholecystectomy, significant alcohol use, or history of pancreatitis.
keyPoints: Loperamide is first-line therapy for IBS-D at 2–4 mg every 4–6 hours, max 12 mg/day.
Loperamide increases anal pressures and delays fecal transit without causing tolerance.
Eluxadoline approved by FDA for IBS-D; mixed opioid receptor activity with minimal systemic bioavailability.
Avoid eluxadoline in patients with prior cholecystectomy, significant alcohol use, or history of pancreatitis.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTISPASMODICS | ANTIDIARRHEAL AGENTS

items.title: Use Osmotic Laxatives for Chronic Constipation with Caution in Renal Impairment
items.content: Osmotic laxatives, such as magnesium citrate and sodium phosphate-based products, are effective in improving symptoms of chronic constipation by drawing fluid into the intestinal lumen. However, caution is advised when using these agents in patients with renal impairment due to risks like severe hypermagnesemia from magnesium hydroxide or electrolyte disturbances from frequent sodium phosphate use.
keyPoints: Osmotic laxatives improve chronic constipation symptoms better than placebo.
Chronic use of magnesium hydroxide may cause severe hypermagnesemia in renal impairment.
Frequent sodium phosphate-based bowel cleansing can lead to hyperphosphatasemia, hypocalcemia, and hypokalemia.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · OSMOTIC LAXATIVES | ANTIFLATULENCE THERAPY

items.title: Polyethylene Glycol (PEG) is Superior for Bowel Preparation and IBS Symptom Relief
items.content: Polyethylene glycol (PEG)-based solutions are iso-osmotic laxatives that induce bowel movements through high-volume lavage. PEG has been shown to be more effective than placebo in inducing bowel movements, with a Cochrane review indicating its superiority over lactulose for improving stool frequency and abdominal pain in IBS patients.
keyPoints: PEG consistently induced more bowel movements than placebo across 19 trials.
Cochrane review of 10 randomized trials showed PEG superior to lactulose for stool frequency and abdominal pain improvement.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · OSMOTIC LAXATIVES | ANTIFLATULENCE THERAPY

items.subtopic: ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES
items.title: Tricyclic Antidepressants in IBS: Mechanism and Efficacy
items.content: Tricyclic antidepressants, such as imipramine, are effective for all IBS subtypes by slowing gut transit and potentially altering visceral afferent neural function. Their benefits on bowel symptoms occur faster and at lower doses than their effects on mood.
keyPoints: Imipramine slows jejunal migrating motor complex transit propagation.
Effective in all IBS subtypes, independent of depression effects.
Therapeutic benefits for bowel symptoms occur faster and at a lower dosage.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES

items.subtopic: ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES
items.title: Tegaserod: Efficacy and Cardiovascular Risks in IBS-C
items.content: Tegaserod, a 5-HT₄ receptor agonist, improves symptoms of IBS-C but was withdrawn due to cardiovascular risks. It is now approved for women under 65 without significant cardiovascular risk factors.
keyPoints: Reduces abdominal discomfort and improves constipation and bloating in >4000 IBS-C patients.
Withdrawn after observing cardiovascular complications (0.11% vs 0.01% placebo).
FDA-approved for women <65 years without significant cardiovascular risk factors.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES

items.subtopic: ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES
items.title: Secretagogues in IBS-C: Mechanism and FDA Approval
items.content: Secretagogues like lubiprostone, linaclotide, and plecanatide enhance intestinal transit by stimulating ion and water secretion. They are effective in treating IBS-C with minimal absorption and side effects.
keyPoints: Lubiprostone activates type 3 chloride channels; dose: 24 mcg twice daily.
Linaclotide (290 or 145 µg once daily) reduces constipation and pain, FDA-approved.
Plecanatide (3-mg once daily) effective in IBS-C, FDA-approved, diarrhea <5%.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES

items.title: Rifaximin for IBS-D: Efficacy and FDA Approval
items.content: Rifaximin, a nonabsorbed oral antibiotic, has been shown to be effective in treating IBS-D. In two large double-blind, placebo-controlled studies with over 1200 patients, rifaximin at 550 mg three times daily for 2 weeks significantly improved symptoms compared to placebo.
keyPoints: Rifaximin is approved by the FDA for IBS-D treatment.
Significant symptom relief in >1200 nonconstipated IBS patients with rifaximin.
Patients responding to rifaximin are more likely to respond to subsequent courses upon relapse.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · MODULATION OF GUT FLORA

items.title: Prebiotics and Their Limited Role in IBS Management
items.content: Prebiotics, which stimulate bacterial growth in the GI tract, have not shown consistent benefits for IBS symptoms. Three out of four randomized trials reported worsening or no improvement in symptoms.
keyPoints: Three studies found prebiotics worsened or did not improve IBS symptoms.
High-carbohydrate diets can adversely affect IBS symptoms.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · MODULATION OF GUT FLORA

items.title: Low FODMAP Diet: Efficacy and Implementation in IBS Management
items.content: A low FODMAP diet reduces IBS symptoms in 50–80% of patients. It involves a three-step process: restriction, reintroduction, and maintenance phases. This dietary intervention is superior to others for reducing global IBS symptoms.
keyPoints: Reduces IBS symptoms in 50–80% of patients.
Superior to other dietary interventions in network meta-analysis.
Involves a three-step process: restriction, reintroduction, and maintenance phases.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · MODULATION OF GUT FLORA

items.question: According to the Rome IV criteria, how often must recurrent abdominal pain occur for it to be considered part of IBS diagnosis?
options: At least once per month in the last 3 months
At least once per week in the last 3 months
At least twice per week in the last 6 months
Daily for at least one month
items.explanation: The Rome IV criteria specify that recurrent abdominal pain must occur, on average, at least once per week in the last 3 months.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a criterion for IBS diagnosis according to the Rome IV criteria?
options: Associated with a change in frequency of stool
Associated with a change in form (appearance) of stool
items.explanation: IBS is characterized by the absence of detectable structural abnormalities.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome

items.question: What percentage of adults and adolescents globally have symptoms consistent with IBS?
items.explanation: Approximately 10% of adults and adolescents worldwide exhibit symptoms consistent with IBS.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a diagnostic criterion for IBS according to Rome IV?
options: Abdominal pain associated with defecation or change in stool frequency/form.
Abdominal pain occurring at least once a week.
Discomfort as one of the criteria.
items.explanation: Rome IV eliminated 'discomfort' as a diagnostic criterion for IBS.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: Which symptom is NOT commonly associated with IBS?
options: Malnutrition due to inadequate caloric intake.
Sleep deprivation due to nocturnal pain.
Worsening symptoms during premenstrual and menstrual phases in females.
Pain exacerbated by eating or emotional stress.
items.explanation: Sleep deprivation is unusual because abdominal pain with IBS is almost uniformly present only during waking hours.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: What percentage of patients with IBS change subtypes over one year?
items.explanation: 75% of patients change subtypes and 29% switch between IBS-C and IBS-D over a year.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: Which statement is true about gas and flatulence in IBS?
options: Most patients with IBS generate more than the normal amount of intestinal gas.
Patients with IBS have impaired transit and intolerance to intestinal gas loads.
Bloating in IBS is always associated with increased abdominal girth.
Gas reflux from distal to proximal intestine does not occur in IBS.
items.explanation: Most patients with IBS have impaired transit and intolerance of intestinal gas loads, even though they generate a normal amount of gas.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: Which condition is associated with increased rectosigmoid motor activity for up to 3 hours after eating in IBS patients?
items.explanation: Increased rectosigmoid motor activity for up to 3 hours after eating is observed in IBS patients, particularly those with diarrhea (IBS-D).
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: What does visceral hypersensitivity in IBS patients suggest about their sensory responses?
options: They exhibit heightened sensitivity to somatic pathways.
They have a nutrient-dependent exaggerated sensory component of the gastrocolonic response.
Their gut sensitivity is similar to that of healthy controls.
Visceral hypersensitivity does not affect IBS symptoms.
items.explanation: Visceral hypersensitivity in IBS patients suggests a nutrient-dependent exaggerated sensory component of the gastrocolonic response, leading to postprandial symptoms.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which brain region shows greater activation in IBS patients during distal colonic stimulation?
items.explanation: The mid-cingulate cortex shows greater activation in IBS patients during distal colonic stimulation, associated with attention processes and response selection.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: What is a common feature of postinfective IBS?
options: It occurs more commonly in males.
It often starts after an acute gastroenteritis-like illness.
It is associated with viral infections.
items.explanation: Postinfective IBS often begins after an acute 'gastroenteritis-like' illness, affecting younger patients more commonly than older ones.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which cytokines are abnormally released by peripheral blood mononuclear cells in IBS patients?
items.explanation: Peripheral blood mononuclear cells from IBS patients show abnormal release of proinflammatory cytokines such as interleukin (IL) 6, IL-1β, and tumor necrosis factor (TNF).
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: What is the role of mast cells in IBS pathogenesis?
options: They are involved in mucosal inflammation and pain
items.explanation: Mast cell activation correlates with the frequency and severity of abdominal pain, suggesting their involvement in mucosal inflammation and visceral hypersensitivity.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Which TRP channel is implicated in the pathogenesis of visceral hypersensitivity in IBS?
items.explanation: Transient receptor potential (TRP) cation channels such as TRPV1 are central to the initiation and persistence of visceral hypersensitivity in IBS.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: What is a common finding regarding intestinal permeability in patients with IBS?
options: Decreased permeability in all IBS subtypes
Impaired barrier function primarily in IBS-D and postinfectious IBS
items.explanation: The intestinal barrier is impaired primarily in patients with IBS-D (diarrhea-predominant) and postinfectious IBS.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Which enzyme is the rate-limiting step in serotonin biosynthesis by enterochromaffin cells?
items.explanation: Tryptophan hydroxylase 1 (TPH1) is the rate-limiting enzyme in enterochromaffin cell serotonin biosynthesis.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.question: What effect do short-chain fatty acids have on colonic serotonin production?
options: They have no effect on serotonin production
items.explanation: Gut microbes promote colonic serotonin production through an effect of short-chain fatty acids on enterochromaffin cells.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.question: What is a consequence of downregulated mucosal serotonin reuptake transporter (SERT) in IBS patients?
options: No change in mucosal serotonin levels
items.explanation: The expression of mucosal serotonin reuptake transporter (SERT) is downregulated due to gram-negative gut dysbiosis, leading to abnormal mucosal serotonin levels.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.question: Which criteria were published in 2016 for the diagnosis of IBS?
items.explanation: The Rome IV criteria, published in 2016, are used for diagnosing IBS based on abdominal pain and altered bowel habits.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following symptoms argues against the diagnosis of IBS?
items.explanation: The presence of nocturnal diarrhea is a symptom that argues against the diagnosis of IBS.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 1)

items.question: What should be checked in patients with IBS-D even without alarm features to rule out IBD?
options: Stool examination for ova and parasites
items.explanation: Fecal calprotectin (or fecal lactoferrin) should be checked in patients with IBS-D to rule out IBD.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a clinical feature suggestive of IBS?
items.explanation: Progressive deterioration over time is not a clinical feature suggestive of IBS.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 1)

items.subtopic: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 2) | PATIENT COUNSELING AND DIETARY ALTERATIONS | STOOL-BULKING AGENTS
items.question: Which type of dietary fiber is often found to be effective in managing IBS symptoms?
options: Insoluble dietary fiber such as wheat bran
Soluble fibers such as psyllium preparations
items.explanation: Soluble fibers such as psyllium preparations are often found to be effective in managing IBS symptoms.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 2) | PATIENT COUNSELING AND DIETARY ALTERATIONS | STOOL-BULKING AGENTS

items.subtopic: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 2) | PATIENT COUNSELING AND DIETARY ALTERATIONS | STOOL-BULKING AGENTS
items.question: What is the recommended starting approach when introducing fiber supplementation in IBS patients?
options: Start with a high dose and decrease as tolerated
Begin at a nominal dose and gradually increase over several weeks
Administer only soluble fibers immediately at full dose
Avoid any form of dietary fiber due to potential side effects
items.explanation: Fiber should be started at a nominal dose and slowly titrated up as tolerated.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 2) | PATIENT COUNSELING AND DIETARY ALTERATIONS | STOOL-BULKING AGENTS

items.subtopic: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 2) | PATIENT COUNSELING AND DIETARY ALTERATIONS | STOOL-BULKING AGENTS
items.question: Which symptom is NOT typically improved by fiber supplementation in IBS patients?
items.explanation: Most studies observe no response in patients with diarrhea or pain-predominant IBS.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 2) | PATIENT COUNSELING AND DIETARY ALTERATIONS | STOOL-BULKING AGENTS

items.question: Which antispasmodic is preferred by some physicians due to fewer side effects on mucous membrane secretion?
items.explanation: Some physicians prefer synthetic anticholinergics like dicyclomine for fewer side effects on mucous membrane secretion.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTISPASMODICS | ANTIDIARRHEAL AGENTS

items.question: For which condition is peppermint oil found to be significantly superior to placebo?
items.explanation: Peppermint oil was found to be significantly superior to placebo for global improvement of IBS symptoms and reduction in abdominal pain.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTISPASMODICS | ANTIDIARRHEAL AGENTS

items.question: What is the initial therapy of choice for IBS-D?
items.explanation: Peripherally acting opiate-based agents, such as loperamide, are the initial therapy of choice for IBS-D.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTISPASMODICS | ANTIDIARRHEAL AGENTS

items.question: Which osmotic laxative is iso-osmotic and induces bowel movement by high-volume lavage?
items.explanation: Polyethylene glycol (PEG)-based solution is iso-osmotic and induces bowel movement by high-volume lavage.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · OSMOTIC LAXATIVES | ANTIFLATULENCE THERAPY

items.question: What potential adverse effect can result from the chronic use of magnesium hydroxide in patients with renal impairment?
items.explanation: Chronic use of magnesium hydroxide may result in severe hypermagnesemia in patients with renal impairment.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · OSMOTIC LAXATIVES | ANTIFLATULENCE THERAPY

items.question: Which dietary intervention has been shown to be effective in reducing gas and bloating?
items.explanation: A low FODMAP diet has been shown to be quite effective in reducing gas and bloating.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · OSMOTIC LAXATIVES | ANTIFLATULENCE THERAPY

items.subtopic: ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES
items.question: Which antidepressant medication has been shown to slow jejunal migrating motor complex transit propagation in IBS-D patients?
items.explanation: Imipramine, a tricyclic antidepressant, slows jejunal migrating motor complex transit propagation in IBS-D patients.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES

items.subtopic: ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES
items.question: What is the primary mechanism by which secretagogues like lubiprostone enhance intestinal transit?
options: They stimulate peristalsis via 5-HT₄ receptor agonism.
They increase chloride and water secretion into the intestinal lumen.
items.explanation: Secretagogues like lubiprostone enhance intestinal transit by increasing chloride and water secretion into the intestinal lumen.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES

items.subtopic: ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES
items.question: Which drug was withdrawn from the market in 2007 due to cardiovascular complications but later approved for use under specific conditions?
items.explanation: Tegaserod was withdrawn from the market in 2007 due to cardiovascular complications but later approved for use under specific conditions.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES

items.subtopic: ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES
items.question: Which of the following is a small-molecule inhibitor of GI sodium-hydrogen exchange-3 approved for IBS-C?
items.explanation: Tenapanor is a small-molecule inhibitor of GI sodium-hydrogen exchange-3 approved for IBS-C.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES

items.question: Which antibiotic has been most thoroughly studied for the treatment of nonconstipated IBS patients?
items.explanation: Rifaximin is the most thoroughly studied antibiotic for treating nonconstipated IBS patients.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · MODULATION OF GUT FLORA

items.question: What percentage of IBS patients typically experience symptom relief with a low FODMAP diet?
items.explanation: A low FODMAP diet reduces symptoms in 50–80% of IBS patients.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · MODULATION OF GUT FLORA

items.question: What is the FDA-approved indication for rifaximin in the treatment of IBS?
items.explanation: Rifaximin is approved by the FDA for the treatment of IBS-D.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · MODULATION OF GUT FLORA

items.question: Which dietary intervention has been found superior in reducing global IBS symptoms according to a network meta-analysis?
items.explanation: A low FODMAP diet was found superior in reducing global IBS symptoms.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · MODULATION OF GUT FLORA

items.statement: IBS is diagnosed based on clear diagnostic markers.
items.explanation: There are no clear diagnostic markers for IBS; diagnosis is based on clinical presentation.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome

items.statement: The Rome IV criteria were introduced in 2016 to update the previous Rome III criteria.
items.explanation: In 2016, the Rome III criteria for IBS diagnosis were updated to Rome IV.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome

items.statement: Painful diarrhea or constipation fulfills the diagnostic criteria for IBS.
items.explanation: Painless diarrhea or constipation does not fulfill the diagnostic criteria to be classified as IBS.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: The prevalence of IBS is higher among patients with dyspepsia than those without.
items.explanation: The prevalence of IBS is indeed higher among patients with dyspepsia (31.7%) compared to those reporting no symptoms of dyspepsia (7.9%).
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: IBS patients exhibit heightened sensitivity to visceral and somatic pathways.
items.explanation: IBS patients show heightened sensitivity selectively for visceral innervation, not for somatic pathways.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: Psychological factors do not influence pain thresholds in IBS patients.
items.explanation: Psychological factors do influence pain thresholds in IBS patients, as stress alters sensory thresholds.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: An abnormal H2 breath test is a definitive predictor of treatment response in IBS patients.
items.explanation: The role of testing for small-intestinal bacterial overgrowth using an abnormal H2 breath test in predicting treatment response remains unclear.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.statement: Mast cell activation is linked to increased antigen presentation and altered enteric function in IBS.
items.explanation: Gut dysbiosis, along with genetic susceptibility and environmental factors, may lead to mast cell activation, altering mucosal permeability and enteric neuronal/smooth-muscle function.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.statement: Increased colonic bile acid levels can lead to accelerated colonic transit and visceral hypersensitivity in IBS-D patients.
items.explanation: Increased colonic bile acid levels influence bowel habit by accelerating colonic transit, intestinal permeability, and visceral hypersensitivity.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.statement: Functional TPH1 polymorphism is associated with all subtypes of IBS.
items.explanation: Functional TPH1 polymorphism has been shown to be associated with specific IBS subtypes, not all.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.statement: The diagnosis of IBS relies on pathognomonic abnormalities.
items.explanation: IBS is diagnosed based on clinical features and exclusion of other diseases, as no pathognomonic abnormalities have been identified.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 1)

items.statement: Stool examination for ova and parasites is necessary for all patients with IBS-D.
items.explanation: Stool examination for ova and parasites is not required for all IBS-D patients but may be considered in regions where parasitic infections are common.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 1)

items.subtopic: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 2) | PATIENT COUNSELING AND DIETARY ALTERATIONS | STOOL-BULKING AGENTS
items.statement: Controlled trials of dietary fiber in IBS patients have consistently shown positive results across all types of IBS.
items.explanation: Controlled trials of dietary fiber in IBS patients have produced variable results due to the heterogeneity of the disorder.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 2) | PATIENT COUNSELING AND DIETARY ALTERATIONS | STOOL-BULKING AGENTS

items.subtopic: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 2) | PATIENT COUNSELING AND DIETARY ALTERATIONS | STOOL-BULKING AGENTS
items.statement: Soluble fibers such as psyllium can help reduce perception of rectal distention in IBS patients.
items.explanation: Fiber supplementation with psyllium has been shown to reduce the perception of rectal distention.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 2) | PATIENT COUNSELING AND DIETARY ALTERATIONS | STOOL-BULKING AGENTS

items.statement: Anticholinergic drugs should be administered after meals to manage postprandial pain effectively.
items.explanation: Antispasmodics are best given 30 minutes before meals for effective management of postprandial pain.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTISPASMODICS | ANTIDIARRHEAL AGENTS

items.statement: Eluxadoline is contraindicated in patients with a history of pancreatitis.
items.explanation: Eluxadoline should not be prescribed for patients with prior cholecystectomy, significant alcohol use, or a history of pancreatitis due to the risk.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTISPASMODICS | ANTIDIARRHEAL AGENTS

items.statement: Sorbitol is more effective than lactulose as a laxative.
items.explanation: Among the nonabsorbable carbohydrates, lactulose and sorbitol had similar laxative effects.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · OSMOTIC LAXATIVES | ANTIFLATULENCE THERAPY

items.statement: Antibiotics may be beneficial for all IBS patients with bloating.
items.explanation: Antibiotics may help in a subgroup of IBS patients with predominant symptoms of bloating.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · OSMOTIC LAXATIVES | ANTIFLATULENCE THERAPY

items.subtopic: ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES
items.statement: The therapeutic benefits of tricyclic antidepressants in IBS are primarily due to their effects on depression.
items.explanation: The beneficial effects of tricyclic antidepressants in IBS appear to be independent of their effects on depression.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES

items.subtopic: ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES
items.statement: Linaclotide and plecanatide are approved by the FDA for IBS-C treatment.
items.explanation: Both linaclotide and plecanatide have been approved by the FDA for the treatment of IBS-C.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES

items.statement: Probiotics are recommended by the American Gastroenterological Association for treating all patients with IBS.
items.explanation: The American Gastroenterological Association makes no recommendations for probiotics due to lack of high-quality data.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · MODULATION OF GUT FLORA

items.statement: A low FODMAP diet involves an initial restriction phase followed by a reintroduction and maintenance phase.
items.explanation: The low FODMAP diet is implemented in a three-step process: restriction, reintroduction, and maintenance phases.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · MODULATION OF GUT FLORA

items.assertion: IBS can be classified into IBS-C, IBS-D, and IBS-M based on bowel habits.
items.reason: Bowel pattern subtypes in IBS are highly unstable over time.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; however, the instability of bowel patterns is not the basis for classification.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.assertion: IBS symptoms are prevalent in other functional GI disorders.
items.reason: Functional dyspepsia and IBS may be manifestations of a single digestive system disorder.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.assertion: Central neural dysregulation is implicated in the pathogenesis of IBS.
items.reason: Functional brain imaging studies show greater activation of specific brain regions in response to visceral stimulation in IBS patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion regarding central neural dysregulation's role in IBS.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Postinfective IBS is more common in older patients.
items.reason: The risk of developing postinfective IBS increases with age.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because postinfective IBS affects younger rather than older patients, though the reason itself may be true.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Increased expression of TRPV1 channels correlates with visceral hypersensitivity in IBS.
items.reason: Mucosal inflammation leads to increased expression and/or sensitization of TRPV1, which is central to the initiation and persistence of visceral hypersensitivity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.assertion: All IBS patients have increased intestinal permeability.
items.reason: Altered intestinal permeability is primarily described in IBS-D and postinfectious IBS, not all subtypes.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because increased intestinal permeability is not universally present across all IBS subtypes.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.assertion: Increased release of serotonin contributes to postprandial symptoms in IBS-D patients.
items.reason: Serotonin plays an important role in the regulation of GI motility and visceral perception.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.assertion: A careful history and physical examination can help establish the diagnosis of IBS.
items.reason: IBS is diagnosed by recognizing clinical features and ruling out other organic diseases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why a careful history and physical exam are helpful.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 1)

items.assertion: Endocrinopathies such as hypothyroidism should be considered in the differential diagnosis of IBS.
items.reason: Hypothyroidism can present with symptoms similar to those of IBS, including constipation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why hypothyroidism should be considered in differential diagnosis.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · Irritable Bowel Syndrome (part 1)

items.assertion: Loperamide is less addictive than paregoric, codeine, or tincture of opium.
items.reason: The intestines do not become tolerant to the antidiarrheal effect of opiates like loperamide.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why loperamide is less addictive.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTISPASMODICS | ANTIDIARRHEAL AGENTS

items.subtopic: ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES
items.assertion: Tegaserod is a prokinetic agent that stimulates peristalsis.
items.reason: It acts as a 5-HT₄ receptor agonist, which enhances gastrointestinal motility.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES

items.subtopic: ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES
items.assertion: SSRIs are highly effective in treating all subtypes of IBS.
items.reason: Their efficacy is well established and consistent across various studies.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; SSRIs have conflicting results and significant heterogeneity among trials.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · ANTIDEPRESSANT DRUGS | SEROTONIN RECEPTOR MODULATORS | SECRETAGOGUES

items.assertion: Prebiotics are effective in improving IBS symptoms.
items.reason: Three out of four studies reported that prebiotics worsened or did not improve IBS symptoms.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because most studies showed no improvement or worsening of symptoms with prebiotics.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · MODULATION OF GUT FLORA

items.assertion: Rifaximin is beneficial for IBS-D patients.
items.reason: Patients with IBS-D who respond to rifaximin are more likely to respond to subsequent courses if symptoms relapse.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why rifaximin is beneficial for IBS-D patients.
items.reference: Irritable Bowel Syndrome · MODULATION OF GUT FLORA
